responseSet.title: Autonomy and Rigid Character: David Shapiro. New York: Basic Books, 1981, 179 pp., $9.95.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Autonomy and Rigid Character

David Shapiro. New York: Basic Books, 1981, 179 pp., $9.95.

Seymour Fisher PHD

Seymour Fisher

Seymour Fisher  

Contemporary thought and research in psychology are heavily saturated with curiosity about the self. There is heightened interest in such matters as the origins of self-regarding attitudes, the effects of focusing attention upon oneself as compared to nonself objects, and the manner in which self-structures serve as selective lenses. The literature teems with topics like narcissism, self-concept, mirror effects, and ego boundaries. Shapiro's book is really an essay about the nature of selfhood. It explores the problems of becoming an independent self and the desperate maneuvers that individuals may resort to in an effort to patch up rents in that abode. To be more specific, Shapiro addresses two issues. First, he explores the degree to which we are actually in charge of ourselves. Second, he outlines the presumed consequences of attempting to defend one's self-territory too rigidly.

It should be said that his method of inquiry is reminiscent of Freud's. He presents elegant theoretical notions, in a skillful literary style; illustrates his points with clinical vignettes; sometimes offers indirect documentation through observations made by others (e.g., Piaget); and basically counts on a line of logical arguments to pull together the skein. He has original ideas and it is exciting to follow along with his analyses. However, he seems to be quite uninterested in the reservoir of research data potentially pertinent to a number of his formulations. I fault him for this but at the same time I can see venturous advantages to the somewhat heroic “I can find my way alone” strategy that he uses to attack knotty problems. In actuality, certain of his arguments could have been nicely bolstered by specific empirical research findings. For example, he asks us to view paranoid behavior as highly motivated by conflicts about power; he could have fortified his position by citing the research of Wolowitz (1965) and others.

Let us turn to Shapiro's first major theme.

[[RunningHead]]

The Legacy of the Holocaust: Psychohistorical Themes in the Second Generation

Robert M. Prince. Ann Arbor, MI: UMI Research Press, 1985, 223 pp., $39.95.

Marcia Pollak PHD

Marcia Pollak

Marcia Pollak  

The concept that the Holocaust is a unique event in human history has been seriously debated. Is the Holocaust comparable to savage massacres of other groups at other times in civilization? Or is it an event that, because of its enormity, transcends all other barbarities in modern history? If it is unique, then this uniqueness must attach itself to the identity of the children of the survivors of the Holocaust. Prince addresses this issue of identity in The Legacy of the Holocaust.

He conceived of his study in the early 1970s, before there was much interest in the children of Holocaust survivors. It is therefore of historical import and a seminal contribution to a standard collection of works on the Holocaust. Prince's doctoral dissertation, on which this text is based, influenced subsequent work, both methodologically and conceptually. The writing is rich and powerful. Especially striking is the sense of two times coexisting, the past richly evident in the present.

Prince identifies three significant sources of influence in the lives of these children: the parents' character style and their relationship with their children, the active participation of the children in imagery from their parents' past, and the cultural context in which the children have been raised. How these influences interweave and transform each other is a major focus of the study. The second focus is the relationship between the children of Holocaust survivors “and the events of history, involving observation of their adaptation to history and anticipation of their effect on it” (p. 15).

For his study, Prince developed a set of questions that were inspired by Lifton's (1969) Psychohistorical Interview. The questions appear properly open ended; they do not force choices.

responseSet.title: Extenders or Modifiers: A Discussion of Weiss and Sampson's Control-Mastery Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Extenders or Modifiers

A Discussion of Weiss and Sampson's Control-Mastery Theory

Doris Silverman PHD

Doris Silverman

Doris Silverman  

Weiss and Sampson are engaged in shaping an innovative theory. They link their ideas to Freud's later ego and super-ego theory. This linkage obscures their unique contributions. In this article I discuss their distinctive theoretical ideas and clinical modifications and contrast these with Freudian theory as well as some other theoretical perspectives. I consider six themes from Weiss and Sampson's work which I believe are innovative departures from Freud: (a) the ego as a motivator of behavior or Weiss and Sampson's object relations perspective, (b) the link between ego motives and attachment research, (c) the nature of the object relational tie, (d) psychic reality and actuality, (e) some clinical implications, and (f) a philosophical assumption about people which underlies their work.

responseSet.title: Ego Boundary Impairment and Thought Disorder in Frequent Nightmare Sufferers
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ego Boundary Impairment and Thought Disorder in Frequent Nightmare Sufferers

Ross Levin PHD

Ross Levin

Ross Levin  

This investigation examined the proposed relationship between nightmare frequency and ego boundary impairment (Hartmann, 1984) in a sample of female college students. Thirty frequent nightmare sufferers (at least one attack per week) and 30 controls (maximum of one nightmare per year) were compared. In order to measure ego boundary impairment, the Rorschach inkblot test was administered and scored for barrier and penetration responses (Fisher & Cleveland, 1968) and indices of thought disorder (Blatt & Ritzler, 1974). An ego boundary questionnaire developed by Hartmann and colleagues (Hartmann, 1989; Hartmann et al., 1987) was also administered. Although barrier and penetration scores did not discriminate, the frequent-nightmare subjects demonstrated more disordered thinking on the Rorschach (p < .001) and greater boundary impairment on the questionnaire (p < .001), providing empirical support for Hartmann's nightmare hypothesis. The results are discussed within the ramework of psychoanalytic object-relations theory.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Psychology: An Argument for Change in Editorial Policy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Psychology

An Argument for Change in Editorial Policy

Irene Fast PHD

Irene Fast

Irene Fast  

In an Editorial Note (Psychoanalytic Psychology, 1989) the Editorial Board of Psychoanalytic Psychology invites comment on its proposal for an occasional section devoted to reports of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. This modification in editorial policy is welcome though it seems a very small advance in the direction of much needed change.

I believe that psychoanalytic psychology is best served by the exploration of its concepts from the broadest possible range of perspectives. It is in the lively interplay of ideas from various sources that concepts and practices are expanded and refined. Valuable heterogeneity in orientation may be stimulated by the differences in interest and training particular to various professions and disciplines interested in psychoanalysis. They may arise in clinical work of greater and less intensity, with adults and children, and in work with the various problems encountered in psychosis, delinquency, neurological impairment and so forth. They may emerge in theoretical explorations of relationships between psychoanalytic and other theories addressing the same phenomena and in empirical studies.

Such a belief argues for an editorial policy that enhances possibilities for interchange in every way possible. It invites a broad definition of psychoanalysis rather than one so restrictive that even psychoanalytic psychotherapy is relegated to the periphery. It suggests welcoming psychoanalytically informed empirical studies. It urges that articles from disciplines other than psychology be considered on an equal footing with those from psychologists.

Requests for reprints should be sent to Irene Fast, PhD, Department of Psychology, 580 Union Drive, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, MI 48109-1346.

responseSet.title: Affect and the Fundamental Nature of Neurosis: Logic and Reality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Affect and the Fundamental Nature of Neurosis

Logic and Reality

Kenneth Isaacs PHD

Kenneth Isaacs

Storm Clinics Evanston, IL

Kenneth Isaacs  

Considering new concepts of affect and affect process, clarifying distinctions among past concepts, and noticing participation of affect dynamics in health and illness suggests an alternative theory of symptom and neurosis. Personality theory based on transient affect has more credibility and has clinical advantage over personality theory based on concepts of enduring affect. I discuss the contrast between the logic and reality of past and new theory, and the inferential processes used. New concepts of affect phobia, affect potentiation, affect storm, affect function, affect process, storm structure, conscious affect, and unconscious affect are presented. The new theory calls for consideration of independent simultaneity rather than sequentiality of affect and cognition, and reconsideration of the place of anxiety in psychology. The theory implies the use of different treatment paradigms for each category of disorder.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Treatment: An Intersubjective Approach

Robert D. Stolorow, Bernard Brandchaft, and George E. Atwood. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1987, 197 pp., $22.50.

Paul Kline PHD DSc

Paul Kline

Paul Kline  

In this book, the authors develop and explicate their well-known, intersubjective approach to psychoanalytic therapy that is derived from the work of Kohut, to whom the book is dedicated. Here, psychoanalytic inquiry is concerned with an indissoluble system—the intersection of two subjectivities, those of patient and analyst—and seeks to illuminate the phenomena within it. The authors argue that their methods facilitate empathic access to their patients' subjective worlds and enhance the scope and therapeutic power of psychoanalysis. They stress that psychoanalysis, thus conceived, is concerned not with knowledge but with subjective reality, and that psychoanalytic interpretations must be evaluated in relation to hermeneutic criteria, such as logical coherence, comprehensiveness of the explanation, consistency with accepted psychological knowledge, and aesthetic beauty. The working out of these principles in analytic practice is then illustrated in a number of interesting clinical case studies that include such difficult problems as borderline and psychotic states.

This is an extremely interesting and well-argued book that, with its carefully chosen clinical cases, appears to demonstrate the value of the intersubjective approach and the integrative power of Kohut's ideas. However, in doing so it raises some fundamental issues in the study and understanding of psychoanalysis both as a theory and as a therapy, and it is these that I want to examine in this review. These issues concern the relation of hermeneutics, science, and the scientific method to the practice, theory, and understanding of psychoanalysis.

Freud, Appraisals and Reappraisals

Contributions to Freud Studies (3 Volumes)

Paul Roazen PHD

Paul Roazen

Paul Roazen  

Freud: Appraisals and Reappraisals: Contributions to Freud Studies, Volume I, edited by Paul E. Stepansky. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1986, 288 pp., $29.95.

Freud: Appraisals and Reappraisals: Contributions to Freud Studies, Volume II, edited by Paul E. Stepansky. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1987, 224 pp., $29.95.

Freud: Appraisals and Reappraisals: Contributions to Freud Studies, Volume III, edited by Paul E. Stepansky. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1987, 224 pp., $29.95.

As psychoanalysis nears its 100th birthday, the relevant scholarship should be far advanced. It is by now widely known how much original documentation about Freud is still sealed up at the Library of Congress at the request of the Freud Archives in New York. Still, the state of interpretative scholarship ought not to be as primitive as it is today. Practicing analysts use Freud for their own purposes, and in most journals passages from Freud are regularly cited anachronistically; little effort goes into trying to understand Freud in his own time, but rather isolated words of his are bandied about in the context of today's therapeutic concerns.

It is in the midst of this regrettable state of affairs that these three volumes edited by Paul E. Stepansky are noteworthy. The various writers here, only a few of whom are clinicians, seek to understand Freud impartially as an object of historical inquiry. Although the essays inevitably suffer from flaws, taken as a whole they represent an admirable shift toward the professionalization of Freud studies.

responseSet.title: Emerging from the “Dark Night of the Soul”: Healing the False Self in a Narcissistically Vulnerable Minister
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Emerging from the “Dark Night of the Soul”

Healing the False Self in a Narcissistically Vulnerable Minister

James Kleiger PsyD

James Kleiger

Bethesda, MD

James Kleiger  

Predisposing narcissistic personality factors and the unique demands of the ministry may combine to create an emotional and spiritual crisis in some members of the clergy. This article examines (a) the concept of selective or inadequate mirroring that can contribute to the development of a false self in narcissistically vulnerable clergy, (b) the unique demands of the ministry that can reinforce the narcissistic minister's sense of falseness, and (c) the importance of the concept of empathic mirroring in the treatment of these patients. A case report describes psychotherapy with a minister whose underlying narcissistic disorder resulted from inadequate mirroring during childhood which set the stage for later disillusionment and despair. The patient's eventual response to empathic selfobjects led not only to a more integrated sense of self but also to a restoration of his faith in a more benign and accepting God. The humanizing effect of psychotherapy is poignantly reflected in his emergence from emotional desolation, his spiritual transformation, and his growing concern for others.

responseSet.title: The Origins of Interpretation in the Countertransference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Origins of Interpretation in the Countertransference

Bruce Lazar Smith PHD

Bruce Lazar Smith

CA

Bruce Lazar Smith  

Although object-relations theory is becoming a dominant paradigm in psychoanalysis, there is as yet no coherent object-relational theory of the therapeutic process. This article attempts to contribute to the development of such a theory by focusing on the processes in the analyst that lead to the formulation of interpretations. Following a review of the countertransference literature, the thesis that all mutative interpretations are first enacted in the countertransference and that therapists gain awareness of the nature of the transference through these enactments is put forth. A series of clinical vignettes is then offered to illustrate this thesis. Finally, the implications of this point of view, for psychoanalytic technique in general, and the concept of neutrality, in particular, are discussed.

responseSet.title: Theories of the Unconscious and Theories of the Self: Raphael Stern. Hillsdale, NJ, The Analytic Press, 1987, 282 pp., $36.00.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Theories of the Unconscious and Theories of the Self

Raphael Stern. Hillsdale, NJ, The Analytic Press, 1987, 282 pp., $36.00.

John Suler PHD

John Suler

John Suler  

An edited book always faces a dilemma. Ideally, it approaches a topic from various perspectives, allowing experts in the field to present their views on the topic and providing a context in which the reader can discover the similarities and differences among those experts' views. However, if the topic is not clearly defined, if the experts address a variety of unrelated issues, then the sense of interlocking perspectives and the push towards synthesis collapses. It is a thin line between a collection of papers that revolve meaningfully around an issue, and a conglomeration of chapters.

Stern's book balances precariously along this line. It is the outcome of a group of conferences sponsored by the Association for Philosophy of Science, Psychotherapy, and Ethics—an organization of linguists, psychotherapists, philosophers, mathematicians, and neurophysiologists. A diversity of contributors can be an asset: Too many cooks do not necessarily spoil the broth. In the case of Stern's book, the downfall comes from this diverse set of experts addressing, as the title makes evident, a very broad topic. Along the wide vista of the psychoanalytic world, what is not subsumed under the concepts of the “unconscious” and the “self”? The book seems to strive for an identity as a psychoanalytic work, or at least attempts to pivot around psychoanalytic themes—but at times even this focus dissolves.

The lead chapter, “The Dynamic Unconscious and the Self,” to which most of the commentaries are addressed, is by Kernberg. The reader must work hard to sift out his penetrating insights from this densely intellectual, abstruse essay.

Editor's Note

In response to requests from our readership and potential contributors, the Editorial Board has indicated interest in determining the possibility of publishing a limited number of distinguished contributions concerning the application of psychoanalysis to short-and long-term psychotherapy. It is expected that these articles will make a contribution to the literature, either in terms of an innovation in technique or in terms of a critical survey of important issues presently confronting the field. These articles will be published in occasional sections of the journal entitled “Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy.” If we are able to obtain a sufficient number of articles, we anticipate at least one such section in each annual volume.

Contributors should explicitly note in the cover letter accompanying their manuscript that this is a contribution to the section on Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy.

The Editorial Board welcomes reader response regarding this addition to the journal.

responseSet.title: Response to Milden's Commentary: Healing the False Self in a Narcissistically Vulnerable Minister
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Milden's Commentary

Healing the False Self in a Narcissistically Vulnerable Minister

Harold Fine

Harold Fine

Knoxville

Harold Fine  

In her response to my review of Lerner's (1985) The Dance of Anger: A Woman's Guide to Changing the Patterns of Intimate Relationships Dr. Milden assumes that I believe that there is no legitimate place for popular presentation of psychological knowledge and that feminist psychoanalytic writings do not represent a serious or thoughtful body of work. Quite the contrary. Although I did not expect The Dance of Anger to be a heavyweight, theoretical treatise, I did expect from someone of Lerner's stature at least a serious-popular book. What she has given us is “kitsch.” This remains paradoxical, for as Milden points out, Lerner has written serious and scholarly works on the subject.

Nor was I judging the book as I would a refereed article. There is a legitimate place for popular articles and books, both feminist and otherwise. And besides Chodorow, Gilligan, Fast, Miller, and Benjamin, the list could include, among others, Viorst, Lewis, and Kaplan. Some of these are scholarly too. Lerner's genre of “pop psychology” devalues our field. I am afraid I understand too well the marketing economy of publishing houses. The accusations of elitism and sexism are knee-jerk responses that curtail dialogue; they smack of Samuel Johnsons riposte about nationalism and patriotism.

There are serious conceptual issues here. Pop psychology reduces our expectations of language and substitutes attitudes and feelings for ideas. Publishing and self-help enterprises have been growth industries for more than a century—witness the proliferation of “recovery” books (one bookstore I visited stacked 91 titles in this area!). Still, encased in the kitsch and jargon are some sound, potentially helpful concepts into how personality develops. In our culture, women have been assigned the primary responsibility for the family's emotional stability, and these codependency, recovery, and “how to” books target these female nurturers. It is almost like a millenium fever—our desire to be reborn.

The reliance on simple, universal techniques to facilitate individual change underscores the belief that we are never victims of circumstance.

[[RunningHead]]

Three Contemporary Theories of Defense and Adaptation

Some Clinical and Theoretical Considerations

Steven Cooper PHD

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper  

Three theories of defense and adaptation by Brenner, Modell, and Kohut are critically appreciated and compared with special reference to the variety of ways in which these concepts have been extended beyond the realm of internal ho-meostasis. Each theory is discussed in terms of its value or limitations in elucidating the theoretical ego or experiential self and object. The three theories are also compared regarding how and to what extent each theory suggests and forecloses lines of interpretation in the clinical context.

The Clinical Diary of Sandor Ferenczi, by S. Ferenczi. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1988, 227 pp., $29.95.

Psychoanalysis: A Theory in Crisis, by M. Edelson. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1988, 392 pp., $39.95.

Psychoanalysis is moving toward a crossroad that has long been mapped into the deepening bifurcation of the two distinct sides of its endeavor: the theoretic and the therapeutic. responseSet.authorMast: Joel Weinberger and  Lloyd H. Silverman
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Testability and Empirical Verification of Psychoanalytic Dynamic Propositions Through Subliminal Psychodynamic Activation

Joel Weinberger PHD and Lloyd Silverman PHD

Lloyd Silverman

Lloyd Silverman  

This article argues that psychoanalysis is a scientifically testable model of human behavior and that at least one research program, “subliminal psychodynamic activation,” has provided support for certain key psychoanalytic propositions. We begin with an attempt to demonstrate the logical feasibility, and even necessity, of testing psychoanalytic propositions in a controlled, extra-clinical setting, followed by a brief explication of the theoretical importance of three types of psychoanalytic propositions. A short history of experimental attempts to demonstrate unconscious influence is then sketched, but the bulk of the article is devoted to reviewing subliminal psychodynamic activation research. We argue that, although gaps remain, the fruits of this research provide support for key psychoanalytic propositions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editors' Preface

James Barron PHD , Joseph Schwartz PHD and Jonathan Slavin PHD

Jonathan Slavin

Jonathan Slavin  

In April, 1989, the Division of Psychoanalysis (39) of the American Psychological Association (APA) held its ninth annual Spring Meeting in Boston. The theme of the conference was: Current Controversies in Psychoanalysis: Theory, Practice, Research and Training. Over a period of 4 days nearly 200 papers (selected from an initial submission of more than 400) covering all aspects of the conference theme were presented. The quality and diversity of the papers represented a dramatic demonstration of how far Division 39 had come in its brief, 9-year existence. Coinciding with the settlement of the lawsuit brought by psychologists against the American Psychoanalytic Association and its co-defendants, the conference and its papers were a celebration of the capacity of psychologists to fully embrace psychoanalysis as their own.

Following the conference, the Board of Directors of Division 39 authorized the publication of a special supplement to the Division's Journal, based on the papers presented at the Spring Meeting. The three guest editors for the supplement were members of the committee which organized the Spring Meeting. In selecting among the papers submitted to us for consideration, we have endeavored to remain true to the theme of the 1989 conference; that is, to frame as clearly as possible the current controversies that enliven our field. We believe that these papers, blind reviewed and selected for overall quality and contribution to knowledge, perform this task well.

Nearly 40 years ago Ernst Kris asserted that psychoanalytic constructs must be modified from time to time in order to retain their usefulness. He described an ideal of “continuous attempts to unify detailed observations by an explanatory framework and to revise the theory thus obtained in the light of new empirical data or impressions” (Kris, 1952, pp. 13-14). In that spirit, the authors of the papers in this special supplementary issue of Psychoanalytic Psychology make detailed observations and look honestly at the implications of their findings. They grapple with data which both confirm and contradict fundamental theoretical assumptions. When they believe the data require revision of those assumptions they boldly attempt necessary reformulation.

[[RunningHead]]

Therapeutic Engagements with Children

Integrating Infant Research and Clinical Practice

Karen Lombardi PHD and Eva Lapidos PHD

Eva Lapidos

Garden City, NY

New york

Eva Lapidos  

This article attempts to integrate recent infant research and psychoanalytic practice with children. From a contemporary theoretical standpoint, babies seem both more agentic and autonomous and more relationally sensitive and responsive than previously thought. When the child is viewed neither as victim and deprivant nor as a bundle of impulses which need to be tamed, but as an agentic force within an interpersonal matrix, our notions of what is central in treatment change accordingly. Particularly central to work with children are the understanding and use of transference and countertransference issues as they emerge both in fantasy play and more directly in relationship with the analyst, and issues of parental projective identification. Illustrative clinical examples are provided.

[[RunningHead]]

Essential Papers on Borderline Disorders: One Hundred Years at the Border

Michael H. Stone. New York: New York University Press, 1986, 580 pp., $55.00.

Ross Levin PHD

Ross Levin

Ross Levin  

Toward the end of his annotated review of the literature in Essential Papers on Borderline Disorders: One Hundred Years at the Border (1986), Stone states,

I am haunted by the dark suspicion that the subject has gotten out of hand. The borderline literature has swollen to a size too vast to be digested by one anthologist. I hope I have made the developments in this field over the past century clearer to the reader than they were before. (p. 432)

Despite the best intentions, the effort to integrate and unify 100 years of discussion and debate on the diffuse diagnosis of borderlines is truly Olympian in its scope.

Stone is an American psychiatrist and psychoanalyst who has written extensively on the borderline patient, including. The Borderline Syndromes (1980). In his writings over the past 15 years, including a 1977 selection in the present volume, Stone has advocated the bio-genetic hypothesis in the etiology of borderline disorders. Much of Stone's research has investigated hereditary links between borderline personality and affective disturbances, particularly bipolar disorder. Given this, it should not be too surprising that many of the readings he has selected, particularly those from the past 10 years, emphasize hereditary aspects of the borderline syndrome.

The volume is divided into four parts arranged chronologically.

responseSet.title: The Idea of History in Psychoanalysis: Freud and the “Wolf-Man”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Idea of History in Psychoanalysis

Freud and the “Wolf-Man”

Robert May PHD

Robert May

Robert May  

The question of history is a lively one in psychoanalysis today. On the one hand, the advocates of narrative (Schafer, 1983; Spence, 1982) question whether there is a history in the positivist sense, and on the other hand the followers of Kohut want to revive the idea that our patients suffer from real past events (Ornstein, 1983). Through a close reading of Freud's “Wolf-Man,” his most extensive clinical examination of the question of history, I elaborate some of what I believe are the inevitable conflicts and contradictions in the psychoanalytic attempt to establish a history. I conclude with some thoughts about why history is so important to us.

responseSet.title: Money Matters and the Woman Analyst: In a Different Voice
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Money Matters and the Woman Analyst

In a Different Voice

Nechama Liss-Levinson PHD

Nechama Liss-Levinson

Nechama Liss-Levinson  

The impact of gender on money matters in psychotherapy is examined. The article suggests that the focus of male and female therapists regarding money issues may differ. The tension between attending to the therapist's own needs and to the needs of the patient are discussed in the context of money issues. Differences in the actual fee and fee policies are examined, with some discussion of the meaning to both patient and analyst of lower fees charged by many women therapists. In addition, numerous clinical vignettes are offered to examine the use of money as a means of interpersonal power and communication. The assymmetries in the structure of the nuclear family are discussed as one possible source of these differing approaches to money. An awareness of one's own attitudes about money, including the culturally assigned sex role, is a prerequisite to understanding the role of money in the therapeutic relationship.

responseSet.title: An Outline of Intersubjectivity: The Development of Recognition
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Outline of Intersubjectivity

The Development of Recognition

Jessica Benjamin PHD

Jessica Benjamin

Jessica Benjamin  

This article formulates an object relations perspective based on the complementarity of intrapsychic and intersubjective aspects of self-development. Object relations theories, even those interested in intersubjectivity, have not followed up on Winnicott's (1971) crucial distinction between the subjectively conceived object and the objectively perceived, outside other. With few exceptions, notably Stern (1985), theorists have overlooked the core element of intersubjectivity, which is mutual recognition. The development of the capacity for mutual recognition can be conceived as a separate trajectory from the internationalization of object relations. The subject gradually becomes able to recognize the other person's subjectivity, developing the capacity for attunement and tolerance of difference. To elaborate on this process, the article discusses differentiation in the first 2 years of life. It suggests that Mahler's rapprochement period can be reinterpretated as a struggle for recognition in which the outcome is not simply object constancy, but the beginning ability to recognize another person's subjectivity. The differentiation process consists not merely of separation, but the continual breakdown and repair of mutuality in the psyche's stance toward the outside. Accepting the inevitability of breakdown and reconstruction of the outside other allows considerable room for the role of intrapsychic fantasy and aggression, which might otherwise be neglected in relational theories of the self.

We are all of us born in moral stupidity, taking the world as an udder to feed our supreme selves: Dorothea had early begun to emerge from that stupidity, but yet it had been easier to her to imagine how she would … become wise and strong in his strength and wisdom, than to conceive with that distinctness which is no longer reflection but feeling … that he had an equivalent center of self, whence the lights and shadows must always fall with a certain difference. (Eliot, 1871/1965, p. 243)

responseSet.title: Disorders of Continuity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disorders of Continuity

Arnold Goldberg MD

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg  

This article begins by delineating the concept of the self as a psychic structure: one which has the property of continuity through time and space. The use of the concept of psychic structure and representation is explicated and defended. Disorders of continuity are characterized as reflecting certain forms of structural pathology and as being manifested in problems of initiating, interrupting, and terminating all manner of behavior. These phenomena become reenacted in the transference phenomena emergent in the analyses of these disorders of continuity. Interpretations are more effectively directed toward the formal components of the structure rather than toward the unconscious contents. Perversion is considered as one form of these disorders, and clinical illustrations are used to demonstrate this problem. The developmental perspective is linked to the activity of interpretation by way of two issues related to continuity: planning ahead and looking backward. These capabilities are part and parcel of normal development. Their absence reflects disorders of continuity, and their place in the interpretive work of analysis allows a structural change that re-establishes this property.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Aspects of Early Gender Development

Toward a Reformulation1

Irene Fast PHD

Irene Fast

Irene Fast  

Freud's profoundly phallocentric perspective distorts not only his conceptions of female gender development but his view of male gender organization and the place of parents in structuring children's femininity and masculinity. Recent explorations of developmental transitions out of infantile narcissism suggest an alternate framework for understanding gender development in both girls and boys. This article addresses the theoretical and clinical implications of this framework for three major phases of gender development, the period prior to children's recognition of sex difference, the period in which they become aware of the differences between the sexes, and the subsequent (oedipal) period.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Supertransference

The Role of the Supervisor's Blind Spots

Stanley Teitelbaum PHD

Stanley Teitelbaum

Stanley Teitelbaum  

In understanding supervisory conflicts and/or stalemates, it is useful to consider not only the role of the therapist's learning problems, but also the supervisor's teaching problems and the sources from which they emanate. This article focuses on the supervisor's contribution to difficulties in the psychoanalytic supervisory situation.

The influence of the supervisor's narcissistic investment and needs, his or her expectations of the therapist, and other aspects of how the supervisor's agenda are gratified or frustrated is explored. The role of conflicts, disappointments, and goals in psychoanalytic supervision is discussed.

responseSet.authorMast: Rand J. Gruen and  Sidney J. Blatt
responseSet.authorCitation: Gruen, R. J. & Blatt, S. J.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Change in Self- and Object Representation During Long-Term Dynamically Oriented Treatment

Rand Gruen PHD and Sidney Blatt PHD

Sidney Blatt

Sidney Blatt  

Recently psychoanalysis has begun to consider how cognitive—affective structures and schemata of self and others, guide and motivate behavior. Impaired and/or distorted representations are assumed to play a significant role in psychopathology. One goal of long-term treatment should be the modification of pathological images of self and other. In this article, we present material from two patients in long-term, dynamically oriented treatment; one, a schizophrenic patient, the other, a patient with a borderline character disorder. Changes in representations of self and others over the course of treatment are examined in conjunction with ongoing independent assessments of changes in the transference in individual therapy. Significant changes in representational structures were closely related to changes taking place in the transference relationship. The findings suggest that growth in representational structures follows a developmental continuum, and that the integration and consolidation of conflicting self-images is dependent on the resolution of central psychological conflicts involving parental representations. The patient's development of a capacity for empathy seems to play an important role in this process.

responseSet.title: The Concept of Mental Representations in Light of Recent Infant Research
responseSet.authorMast: Lawrence Zelnick and  Ester S. Buchholz
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of Mental Representations in Light of Recent Infant Research

Lawrence Zelnick PsyD and Ester Buchholz PHD

Ester Buchholz

Ester Buchholz  

This article juxtaposes psychoanalytic thought with recent infant research toward an understanding of the range of meanings of the term mental representation. A definition of mental representations as unconscious organizing structures of interactions is proposed as a comprehensive integration of the various points of view on this issue, incorporating the traditional psychoanalytic view of representations as well as those revisions suggested by extensions of theory within psychoanalysis and developmental research. An overview of traditional psychoanalytic thought on representations is presented as is a review of later contributions. Several points of divergence between the two bodies of thought are discussed, including the issue of self-object differentiation, the caretaker-child dyad as the focus of analysis, and the capacity within the first year for emergent structures which allow the infant to develop interpersonal expectations. The influences of Piaget's theory of representation on each body of thought are also addressed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Changes in Separation—Individuation and Intersubjectivity in Long-Term Treatment

Diana Diamond PHD , Nadine Kaslow PHD , Sheila Coonerty PHD and Sidney Blatt PHD

Sidney Blatt

Sidney Blatt  

Concepts of separation-individuation (Mahler, Pine, & Bergman, 1975) and intersubjectivity (Stern, 1985) were operationalized in order to develop a methodology for assessing changes in self-and object representations that occur over the course of long-term, psychoanalytically oriented inpatient treatment. Adolescent and young adult patients were asked at admission and every 6 months thereafter until the end of treatment to describe their mother, father, therapist, and themselves. In addition to this Object Representation Inventory (ORI), some patients were also administered a Rorschach both at admission and discharge. The Rorschach data were analyzed based on a separation-individuation scale originally developed by Coonerty (1986) which we revised to include more mature levels of separation-individuation. A parallel scale was also developed to evaluate responses obtained from the ORI. Results indicate that toward the end of treatment, representations of self and other show a clearer sense of boundaries and separateness, as well as a greater degree of empathic relatedness. Four case studies are presented to illustrate these changes.

responseSet.title: The Concepts of Need and Wish in Self Psychology: Healing the False Self in a Narcissistically Vulnerable Minister
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concepts of Need and Wish in Self Psychology

Healing the False Self in a Narcissistically Vulnerable Minister

Morris Eagle PHD

Morris Eagle

Morris Eagle  

Kohut (1984) contrasted self psychology with traditional psychoanalytic theory partly on the basis that the latter understands transference behavior (as well as pathology) in terms of infantile wishes that ultimately need to be renounced, while the former understands transference behavior (and pathology in general) in terms of needs that are to be understood and partially met in the treatment.

The main purpose of this article is to try to show that Kohut's explicit position notwithstanding, a careful examination of the self psychology conception of pathology and of treatment suggests that it is characterized by an implicit reference to wishes rather than simply to needs. I also try to show that an adequate conception of pathology as well as of treatment will involve an understanding of the patient's behavior in terms of the active pursuit of wishes and aims, rather than simply in terms of needs that are the automatic consequences of defective structures.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Relational Concepts in Psychoanalysis

Stephen A. Mitchell. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1988, 306 pp. + references and index, $25.00.

David Scharff MD

David Scharff

Washington, CD

David Scharff  

Stephen Mitchell's book, Relational Concepts in Psychoanalysis, is a landmark statement for psychoanalytic theory, and especially of the place of relational theory. It stands outside and above the field, viewing developments over the century since Freud began his explorations. Mitchell has his own point of view, one introduced in the earlier book he co-authored with Greenberg, Object Relations in Psychoanalysis (Greenberg & Mitchell, 1983). Building on that previous scholarly statement and contrasting of the positions of many psychoanalytic theorists, he now compares each of the major positions of psychoanalytic theory specifically to the new model he proposes, which he calls a “relational-conflict model.” This model is neither the “drive-conflict” model derived centrally from Freud, nor the “developmental-arrest” model that Mitchell associates with Winnicott and Kohut. Mitchell's model is closest to those proposed by Fairbairn and Racker, but he also relies heavily on Sullivan, Loewald, Schafer, and other modern writers who have contributed to a view of the individual as centered in the human environment and interactive with it.

Mitchell has put himself squarely in the middle of a position that many of us embrace. He describes the situation of many analysts who feel their work to be imprisoned in a procrustean bed of “classical theory.” The theoretical bed will not stretch to fit the expanding data about human development, while the patients will not shrink to fit its limited framework. Psychoanalysis is now consumed with the task of locating a new theoretical core.

Requests for reprints should be sent to David E. Scharff, MD, 1610 New Hampshire Avenue Northwest, Washington, DC 20009.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Repression, Accessibility, and the Translation of Private Experience

George Bonanno PHD

George Bonanno

George Bonanno  

This article explores two underlying assumptions of the structural deficits approach to psychoanalytic psychotherapy (i.g., Gedo, 1984; Kohut, 1984); (a) that the analyst must gain a knowledge of the patient's intrapsychic capacities through empathetic processes, and (b) that inaccessible memories are split off or repressed from expression within the analytic hour because the patient lacks the adecuate intrapsychic capacities and structures to tolerate their associated affects. These assumptions are discussed within the context of contemporary views of multiple modes or forms of memory, repression, and the question of accessibility. It is concluded that the mechanisms of repression can operate at the stages of initial encoding as well as the retrieval stage and, consequently, that traumatic memory contents appearing to be relatively inaccessible may be so (a) because of active repression at the time of retrieval or (b) because a minimal number of associative connections have been established with other contents in memory. Thus, a greater portion of private experience is preconsciously available than would be suggested by the deficits approach.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How the Patient's Sense of Danger and Safety Influence the Analytic Process

Harold Sampson PHD

Harold Sampson

Harold Sampson  

Weiss (this issue) has proposed that a person may exercise unconscious control over repressions on the basis of unconscious appraisals of danger and safety. This hypothesis derives from Freud's (1926) theory in which a person institutes repression because of an anticipation that certain inner feelings or intentions would bring about a danger situation. The person's anticipation of danger (based on a pathogenic belief) evokes anxiety and leads to defense. The person maintains repressions and symptoms in obedience to such beliefs and the dangers they foretell.

responseSet.title: Freud's Unwritten Case
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Unwritten Case

Douglas Davis PHD

Douglas Davis

Douglas Davis  

Details of Freud's 5-year treatment of a male patient are reconstructed from the Wilhelm Fliess correspondence. The patient, “E.,” links Freud's therapeutic work and his self-analysis during the transition years between 1895 (Studies on Hysteria) and 1900 (The Interpretation of Dreams). The use of material from “E.” 's therapy in The Interpretation of Dreams—the patient's dream of being arrested for infanticide, and Freud's own dream stemming from criticism of the 5-year length of “E.” 's treatment—links “E.” 's therapy to Freud's own anxiety-neurotic difficulties in the late 1890s and to the nascent theories of neurotic etiology, psychoanalytic interpretation, and transference.

responseSet.title: Higher Cortical Functions and the Ego: Explorations of the Boundary Between Behavioral Neurology, Neuropsychology, and Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Higher Cortical Functions and the Ego

Explorations of the Boundary Between Behavioral Neurology, Neuropsychology, and Psychoanalysis

Douglas Watt PHD

Douglas Watt

Malden, MA

Douglas Watt  

This article explores current thinking about brain organization and the potential bridging of various neuropsychological and psychoanalytic ideas. The focus is on the potentially vital, direct, and generally unappreciated connection of basic neuropsychological ideas to equally basic psychoanalytic constructs, particularly around concepts of drive, affect, and ego, and in terms of the corticolimbic, frontal-posterior, and lateral axes of brain organization. In terms of the corticolimbic axis, I suggest that Freud's basic model of the mind, in which the ego mediates between an internal drive milieu and perceptions of external reality, bears a remarkable resemblance to recent notions in behavioral neurology about the hierarchical organization of the cortex and the limbic system. In terms of the left—right lateral axis of organization, the affective (as opposed to more strictly cognitive) aspects of lateralization are linked to concepts suggesting right hemisphere cognitive processing in transference phenomena. Transference, although clinically complex, has consistent common denominators linking it with right hemisphere functions; it may be universal because it is neuropsychologically elemental. Empirical findings of right hemisphere involvement in affect may be due to the advantages of the right hemisphere's processing style for constructing self- and object images and for processing these images analogically, thus allowing for “preferential” linkage of the right hemisphere to limbic structures. The neuropsychology and basic integrative functions of the frontal cortex are also reviewed in relation to models for higher ego functions and their relationship to adaptation. Possible neuropsychological aspects of “insight” are discussed, and proposals are offered for the further bridging of psychoanalysis, biological psychiatry, and neuroscience.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Interpretation and Psychic Structure

Jay Greenberg PHD

Jay Greenberg

Jay Greenberg  

Models of the mind embody theorists' convictions about the nature of human personality and psychopathology. Freud's various models in particular are based on his changing views of conflict. In this article, I argue that the assumptions about conflict that give rise to the tripartite structural model are too narrow and limiting to encompass what psychoanalysts know clinically today. I spell out what I think these assumptions are and suggest some directions for future theoretical development.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcissism

Reflections on Others' Images of an Elusive Concept

John Auerbach PHD

John Auerbach

John Auerbach  

Narcissistic States and the Therapeutic Process, by Sheldon Bach. New York and London: Aronson, 1985, xix + 253 pp., $25.00.

Humanizing the Narcissistic Style, by Stephen M. Johnson. New York and London: Norton, 1987, xiii + 284 pp., $27.95.

The Many Faces of Shame, edited by Donald L. Nathanson. New York and London: Guilford, 1987, xvi + 370 pp., $30.00.

Of the many concepts that Freud bequeathed us, few have proved as elusive as narcissism. In his first systematic exposition of this concept, Freud (1914) stated that the term narcissism was coined to refer to a paraphilia in which one takes one's own body, rather than another person, as a sexual object. He proceeded, however, to redefine narcissism not as a disorder of sexual object choice but as a normal process, “the libidinal complement to the egoism of the instinct of self-preservation, a measure of which may justifiably be attributed to every living creature” (pp. 73-74). This formulation usually has been rendered as the libidinal cathexis of the ego or, as modified by Hartmann (1950), the libidinal cathexis of the self. Narcissism, according to this essentially economic definition, means self-love and self-esteem. In this framework, narcissism is depleted by libidinal investment in another and is reacquired when one receives love from another or approval from one's ego ideal, itself in turn rooted in narcissism (Freud, 1914).

responseSet.title: The Treatment of Early Object Loss: The Need to Search
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Treatment of Early Object Loss

The Need to Search

Paul Lerner EdD

Paul Lerner

Asheville, NC

Paul Lerner  

In treating certain adult patients who experienced a significant early loss I have observed a particular phenomenon in which the treatment prompted in the patient a compelling need to search in the external world, find, and symbolically reclaim the lost object. Two cases are presented to demonstrate the phenomenon and the delicate interplay between treatment and external search. It is suggested that in recapturing a concrete and direct aspect of the lost object the patient is filling in missing parts of the internal representation of the object and that this is necessary before the patient can fully mourn the object's loss and, in turn, recapture lost aspects of the self.

responseSet.title: On Becoming a Mother: In the Throes of Developmental Transformations
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Becoming a Mother

In the Throes of Developmental Transformations

Paul Trad MD

Paul Trad

Paul Trad  

Parenthood, particularly motherhood, is one of the most common and powerful transformations in human experience. Recent clinical evidence suggests that the physiological and psychological transformations experienced by women while pregnant exert a significant impact on the relationship forged with the infant after birth. After childbirth, the new mother undergoes further transformation as the infant begins to manifest developmental autonomy. However, the cultural induction that women receive which mandates the expression of only positive and nurturing emotions towards the infant may make it difficult for them to express any ambivalent impulses aroused by their infant's burgeoning independence because such feelings are considered socially unacceptable for new mothers. Dreams and fantasies reported by pregnant women and new mothers during these transformation periods offer predictive insight into how the patient will master the challenges posed by motherhood. Five case studies are presented, tracing the development of transformation themes through prepregnancy, pregnancy, and early motherhood.

Toward a New Paradigm for Psychoanalysis

An Evolutionary Biological Perspective on the Classical—Relational Dialectic

Malcolm Slavin PHD and Daniel Kriegman PHD

Daniel Kriegman

Daniel Kriegman  

Psychoanalytic theories can be usefully viewed as derivative of two highly divergent, underlying paradigms: the classical-and ego-psychological traditions on one side, and aspects of object-relational, interpersonal, and self psychological approaches on the other. Attempts to combine these perspectives, though sometimes interesting and useful, typically fail to achieve a true integration. They can be seen as rooted in different visions of human nature,'and have been characterized by several theorists as essentially incompatible (Greenberg & Mitchell, 1983; Modell, 1984). For the first time historically, contemporary evolutionary biology provides us with a perspective on human nature in which these philosophically antithetical, psychoanalytic viewpoints can be substantially incorporated into a broader framework. The resulting “new paradigm” includes crucial elements of both existing traditions, yet fundamentally alters some of the basic premises of

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Nature of the Patient's Problems and How in Psychoanalysis the Individual Works to Solve Them

Joseph Weiss MD

Joseph Weiss

Joseph Weiss  

The patient's psychopathology stems from pathogenic beliefs acquired in childhood from traumatic experiences with parents. The patient's pathogenic beliefs warn the patient that if he or she attempts to gratify certain impulses or to seek certain developmental goals the patient will risk the disruption of his or her all-important parental ties. It is as a consequence of these beliefs that the patient develops fear, anxiety, guilt, shame, or remorse; institutes repressions; and develops symptoms, inhibitions, and faulty object relations. The patient suffers unconsciously from pathogenic beliefs and so is powerfully motivated unconsciously to work with the analyst to disconfirm them. The patient works by testing the beliefs in relation to the analyst in the hope of disconfirming them, and by assimilating insight into the beliefs (and their falseness) conveyed by the analyst's interpretations. Indeed the therapeutic process is in essence the process by which the patient works consciously and unconsciously with the analyst to disconfirm pathogenic beliefs.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Distinction Between Clinical Process and Clinical Content Theories

Emanuel Peterfreund MD

Emanuel Peterfreund

New York

Emanuel Peterfreund  

This article emphasizes, first, the distinction between clinical process theory which refers to the method of investigation, and clinical content theory which refers to the findings of an investigation. It is suggested that psychoanalysts attend to the process, attempt to specify it, and microanalyze it in its naturalistic context. Second, it is suggested that we attend to the problem of clinical evidence, perhaps the most neglected topic in psychoanalytic therapy. Third, the article discusses the significance of broad-based mental models of human experience in understanding the therapeutic process. Fourth, it is suggested that we do best to view psychoanalytic therapy as an early craft, hardly anything that can yet be called science.

The Mysterious Metamorphoses of Bertha Pappenheim

Anna O. Revisited

Rosalea Schonbar PHD and Helena Beatus PHD

Helena Beatus

Helena Beatus  

The life of Bertha Pappenheim (Breuer's “Anna O.”) is examined in an attempt to understand her breakdown at age 21 and her emergence as a creative leader and reformer 8 years later. We believe that understanding any life demands a theory that considers the individual's unique biological, familial, and sociocultural background, recognizes continuity over the lifespan, and contains a principle to account for such continuity. Classical drive theory does not meet these criteria. This article attempts to apply these criteria to Bertha Pappenheim's apparently discontinuous life.

[[RunningHead]]

The Works of Jacques Lacan: An Introduction

Bice Benvenuto and Roger Kennedy. New York: St. Martin's Press, 1986, 237 pp., $27.50.

Norman Holland PHD

Norman Holland

Norman Holland  

Thanks to its clarity, this is a book that allows us to raise the whole issue of Lacan's version of psychoanalysis.

“The aim of this book,” its first sentence says, “is to give a clear introduction to the work of the French psychoanalyst Jacques Lacan” (p. 9). In this, Benvenuto and Kennedy have succeeded admirably. If you are looking for a book to guide your students or yourself through Lacan's version of psychoanalysis, I know of none I would recommend more highly. Lacan's writing is witty and playful, but notoriously difficult. Benvenuto and Kennedy include just enough quotations from Lacan (notably in their chapters 9 and 10) to make one grateful one is reading a summary and not the original.

Lacan is difficult to read because he limits his vocabulary to three fields: (a) the elements of the oedipal stage: father, mother, phallus, vagina, and castration; (b) emotionless words out of the French philosophical stylebook, disembodied, either/or words like: possibility, impossibility, absence, presence, lack, and desire (without a desirer); and (c) puns. With this sensational, if limited, lexicon Lacan discusses neurosis or psychosis (in just those global terms) or hysteria (which has come back into fashion in Lacanian circles). One will look in vain to Lacan for the careful, scientific distinctions of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (3rd ed.; DSM-III; American Psychiatric Association, 1980).

responseSet.title: Comment on Wright, O'Leary, and Balkin's “Shame, Guilt, Narcissism, and Depression: Correlates and Sex Differences”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comment on Wright, O'Leary, and Balkin's “Shame, Guilt, Narcissism, and Depression

Correlates and Sex Differences”

David Harder PHD

David Harder

David Harder  

Wright, O'Leary, and Balkin (1989) have laudably used an empirical approach to increase our understanding of the roles played by shame, guilt, and sex differences in depression and narcissism. However, the lack of construct validity for the guilt scale they used seriously compromises some of their conclusions. At variance with those conclusions, other studies suggest that guilt may not be less involved in depression than shame and that guilt, like shame, may be more common among women than men. Possible dynamics underlying this difference are proposed. Wright et al. also reported that men show more narcissism than women, but it is argued here that such a difference may be overstated. Theoretical implications of these findings and hypotheses are raised. Finally, clinical implications for treatment of shame-prone patients are discussed.

responseSet.title: Paradoxes of Masochism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Paradoxes of Masochism

Anita Katz PHD

Anita Katz

Anita Katz  

Five paradoxes of masochism are presented delineating intrapsychic and interpersonal patterns in which pain and unpleasure, rather than being simply warnings, actually seem to become aims. Elements of the apparent contradictions inherent in masochistic disorders and some of their transference and countertransference implications are discussed. The five paradoxes presented highlight: (a) intrapsychic conflict around issues of drive and defense; (b) conflict between autonomy and gratification through depending on others; (c) pain inherent in the quest for perfection; (d) pain associated with attachment to the object; and (e) pleasure evokes physical pain and injury, a screen for early dire physical trauma. Case material illustrates each paradox.

responseSet.title: One Person and Two Person Psychologies and the Method of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

One Person and Two Person Psychologies and the Method of Psychoanalysis

Lewis Aron PHD

Lewis Aron

Lewis Aron  

The distinction between a monadic theory of mind (a one-person psychology) and a relational theory of mind (a two-person psychology) is crucial in understanding psychoanalytic concepts. However, some psychoanalytic theorists see these two models as essentially complementary whereas others see them as contradictory and irreconcilable.

I argue that the artificial distinction between clinical theory and metapsychology obscures the recognition that the most fundamental psychoanalytic clinical concepts and procedures were formulated and historically understood as one-person phenomena. Transference was not conceptualized as an interpersonal event occurring between two people but was rather understood as a process occurring within the mind of the analysand.

The article attempts to extricate fundamental clinical concepts from the quasibiological drive theory that has dominated both our metapsychology and our clinical theory, and to reexamine the value of these clinical concepts within a relational, contextual, and intersubjective framework. The article examines the method of free association in order to illustrate the different implications of one-person and two-person psychologies. I propose that a two-person or relational field theory does not need to neglect or minimize the intrapsychic, the importance of fantasy, psychic reality, or the centrality of bodily and childhood experience.
